topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my laptop. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I stared at the screen, the familiar, gentle font of his message mocking me. He had tagged me in a photo from the school event—a photo I hadn't even realized he had taken. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the empty room. I paced the small space, my hands flexing into tight fists. Every nerve ending felt frayed, buzzing with a sharp, unpleasant energy. Why? What was he thinking? I couldn't reconcile the composed, patient instructor with this casual digital intrusion. I needed to erase the evidence, to scrub this unwelcome connection from my periphery.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at my phone, the screen illuminating my face with a cold, hard light. Another post from Ms. Evans. She had shared a meme—a slightly sarcastic one about homework—and the caption was a direct reference to a private conversation we’d had in class last Tuesday. A sudden, intense heat bloomed behind my eyes. I felt a tremor run through my hands; I gripped the phone until my knuckles were white. "This is ridiculous," I muttered under my breath, the words tasting bitter. I threw the device onto my desk, the impact jarring. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing to physically distance myself from the source of this irritating awareness. My breath hitched, shallow and fast, as I walked towards the window, trying to breathe in the indifferent afternoon air.

[story 3]

It started innocently enough—a shared group chat for project updates. Then, the pattern solidified. Every time I posted something related to the course, there was a corresponding reaction, a like, or a direct mention from Professor Davies. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench. I watched him across the lecture hall, his expression perfectly neutral, yet I felt a surge of intense agitation radiating from my chest. I couldn't look at him. If I met his eyes, I felt compelled to react, to challenge this invisible boundary he’d crossed. I started tapping furiously on my keyboard, drafting a response that was purely hostile, a torrent of clipped, defensive sentences. My pulse hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding release. I needed to vent this suffocating feeling, to shatter the silence with something sharp and undeniable.
